text: The surgeon carefully dissects the omental flap from the colon, revealing the appendix and small bowel drawn into the area of the fistula. The surgeon identifies the colon to right vaginal fistula and maneuvers to clear the small bowel and appendix for better visualization, while checking for the ureter.

The surgeon identifies the fistula leading into the right side of the vagina, with a segment of small bowel adhered to the cavity. Using robotic instruments, the surgeon mobilizes the small bowel from the retroperitoneum, examining the previous small bowel anastomosis.

The surgeon carefully dissects the appendix, which is adhered to the right ureter and common iliac artery, using ICG-stained stents for guidance. After freeing the appendix, an appendectomy is performed by ligating the base with silk ties and transecting it. The surgeon then proceeds to mobilize the colon, lifting the mesentery from the retroperitoneum, while considering the presence of an abscess and potential residual mesh.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the iliac vessels to avoid presacral bleeding, focusing on the mesentery of the bowel. A right-sided abscess is visible, wrapping posteriorly and entering the vagina. The surgeon identifies the posterior origin of the bowel fistula and shifts to dissecting laterally through less inflamed planes. Using ICG for enhanced visualization, the surgeon lifts the lateral aspect of the old anastomosis off the sacrum, revealing the posterior cavity and the opening of the bowel. The robotic instruments aid in safely lifting the tissue off the sacrum, exposing the deeper pelvic cavity that connects to the vagina.

The surgeon performs a dissection of the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating the vagina from the distal rectum using a camera angled at thirty degrees for optimal visualization. The bladder and vagina are lifted, and a leak test reveals bubbles from the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, indicating the origin of the fistula. The surgeon proceeds with a mesenteric resection off the sacrum, uncovering mesh and tacks that contributed to the fistula formation.

The surgeon staples the bowel just distal to the prior anastomosis, confirmed via proctoscopy. The camera provides a high view as the surgeon carefully extracts mesh and numerous tacks from the surgical site, avoiding bleeding. The posterior cavity, visible wrapping around the right side and extending into the vagina, is shown to the assistant. The surgeon continues to remove the mesh and tacks, noting the excessive number used by the original surgeon.

The surgeon meticulously removes residual fibers of mesh from the pelvic cavity, ensuring no healthy bowel is placed over the area. The pelvic cavity is covered with omentum, and a colostomy is performed. The surgeon marks the short rectal stump with a proline stitch for future identification. The vagina is inspected for repair, but a pinhole fistula is left untouched to avoid further injury. A pelvic drain is placed, and the colostomy is situated in the left upper quadrant.
